content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 007 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660        322-5660         322-5901         322-6441         322-6444

November 16, 1978


Honorable Ken Kawaichi
Oakland Municipal Court
600 Washington Street
Oakland, CA 94607

Dear Judge Kawaichi:

     This letter will confirm our telephone conversation of
October 18, 1978, in which I advised you on the reportability
of a trip provided by the federal government. Specifically,
you and other municipal court judges in Alameda County have
been invited by the federal government to attend a drug and
alcohol abuse seminar being sponsored for the purpose of
learning about federally sponsored drug and alcohol abuse
programs which may be used as alternatives in the sentencing
process from intoxication offenses.

     The seminars will take place in the Monterey area and
will last for several days. The federal government has
offered to pay for all expenses, including food, lodging and
travel, incurred by the judges, spouses and their dependents.

     I informed you that any reimbursements or payments made
by the federal government on your behalf for attending this
seminar would be treated as reimbursements for expenses
incurred in connection with your official duties. Under new
legislation (Ch. 641) applicable to annual Statements of
Economic Interests filed in March 1979, reimbursements for
expenses from the federal government are not income under
the Political Reform Act and consequently need not be reported.

     With respect to payments or reimbursements made on
behalf of your spouse or dependents, it is our view that
they have received a gift. It is the Commission's policy
that unless it is the intent of the donor to influence the
official or make an indirect gift to the official, the gift
is the separate property of the spouse and/or dependent and
need not be reported by the public official. Enclosed for
your consideration is a copy of an opinion issued to Kenneth
Cory, 2 FPPC Opinions 48 (No. 75-094-A, April 22, 1976).

Honorable Ken Kawaichi
November 16, 1978
Page Two


I hope that this information has been helpful. If we
may be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to
contact our office.

Sincerely,

Dwight E. Dickerson
Staff Counsel
Conflicts of Interest Division

DED:ph
Enclosure

sections.question_synthetic: Are reimbursements for expenses paid by the federal government for a municipal court judge attending a drug and alcohol abuse seminar reportable as income under the Political Reform Act, and must gifts provided to the judge's spouse and dependents also be reported?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Reimbursements from the federal government for the judge's own expenses incurred in connection with official duties are not considered income under new legislation (Ch. 641) and need not be reported on the annual Statement of Economic Interests. However, payments made on behalf of the judge's spouse or dependents are treated as gifts to those individuals, and unless intended to influence the official, such gifts are the separate property of the recipient and need not be reported by the judge.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present; content derived from letter body. Document is a response to a telephone conversation on October 13, 1978, confirmed in writing. OCR quality issues present in header and formatting but core advice is clear.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are reimbursements for expenses paid by the federal government for a municipal court judge attending a drug and alcohol abuse seminar reportable as income under the Political Reform Act, and must gifts provided to the judge's spouse and dependents also be reported?

CONCLUSION: Reimbursements from the federal government for the judge's own expenses incurred in connection with official duties are not considered income under new legislation (Ch. 641) and need not be reported on the annual Statement of Economic Interests. However, payments made on behalf of the judge's spouse or dependents are treated as gifts to those individuals, and unless intended to influence the official, such gifts are the separate property of the recipient and need not be reported by the judge.

Specifically, you and other municipal court judges in Alameda County have been invited by the federal government to attend a drug and alcohol abuse seminar being sponsored for the purpose of learning about federally sponsored drug and alcohol abuse programs which may be used as alternatives in the sentencing process from intoxication offenses. The seminars will take place in the Monterey area and will last for several days. The federal government has offered to pay for all expenses, including food, lodging and travel, incurred by the judges, spouses and their dependents. I informed you that any reimbursements or payments made by the federal government on your behalf for attending this seminar would be treated as reimbursements for expenses incurred in connection with your official duties. With respect to payments or reimbursements made on behalf of your spouse or dependents, it is our view that they have received a gift. embedding.summary: FPPC Staff Counsel advises a municipal court judge that federal government reimbursements for his attendance at a drug and alcohol abuse seminar constitute non-reportable expenses under Ch. 641, while gifts provided to his spouse and dependents are not reportable by him under FPPC gift policy.
